Yukihito, Prince Take
[[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] | burial_place = [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]], [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], [[:en:Meguro|Meguro]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese]] [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Lady-in-waiting]] of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]]. A [[:en:Concubine|Concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], she was the mother of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] as the second daughter of imperial [[:en:Chamberlain (office)|chamberlain]] [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]''. The [[:en:Yanagiwara family|Yanagiwara family]] were of the [[:en:Reizei family|Reizei family]] line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, [[:en:Count|Count]][https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. [[:en:Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu]] (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō]] and later Governor of [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]]. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the [[:en:Treaty of Shimonoseki|Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty]] after the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of [[:en:Meningitis|Meningitis]] the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. [[:en:Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|Takagi Yuriko]], married her grandson [[:en:Takahito, Prince Mikasa|Prince Mikasa]].{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the [[:en:Hirohito|Showa Emperor]], having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]] (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Sacred Treasure|Order of the Sacred Treasure]] (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Precious Crown|Order of the Precious Crown]] (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] {{draft categories| }} [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q11573873}}; {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure, 2nd Class|WD=Q56194084}}|given_name={{ill|Naruko|WD=Q110174347}}|resting_place={{ill|Yūten-ji|WD=Q3087698}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8193045]] Wikidata name: Yanagiwara Naruko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Concubine of Emperor Meiji, mother of Emperor Taishō (1859-1943)}} {{family name hatnote|Yanagiwara|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Yanagiwara Naruko | image = Naruko Yanagiwara.png | caption = | title = Imperial Concubine | succession = Imperial Concubine to [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] | reign = 20 February 1873 – 30 July 1912 | birth_date = 26 June 1859 | birth_place = [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1943|10|16|1859|6|26|df=y}} | death_place = | father = Yanagiwara Mitsunaru | mother = | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] | issue = Shigeko, Princess Ume
Yukihito, Prince Take
[[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] | burial_place = [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]], [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], [[:en:Meguro|Meguro]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese]] [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Lady-in-waiting]] of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]]. A [[:en:Concubine|Concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], she was the mother of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] as the second daughter of imperial [[:en:Chamberlain (office)|chamberlain]] [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]''. The [[:en:Yanagiwara family|Yanagiwara family]] were of the [[:en:Reizei family|Reizei family]] line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, [[:en:Count|Count]][https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. [[:en:Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu]] (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō]] and later Governor of [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]]. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the [[:en:Treaty of Shimonoseki|Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty]] after the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of [[:en:Meningitis|Meningitis]] the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. [[:en:Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|Takagi Yuriko]], married her grandson [[:en:Takahito, Prince Mikasa|Prince Mikasa]].{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the [[:en:Hirohito|Showa Emperor]], having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]] (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Sacred Treasure|Order of the Sacred Treasure]] (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Precious Crown|Order of the Precious Crown]] (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] {{draft categories| }} [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222}}|children={{ill|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052}}; {{ill|Shigeko, Princess Ume|WD=Q105711297}}; {{ill|Yukihito, Prince Take|WD=Q11065315}}|father={{ill|Yanagiwara Mitsunaru|WD=Q96200289}}|relatives={{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883}}|family={{ill|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139}}|awards={{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q64735577}}; {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q11573873}}; {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure, 2nd Class|WD=Q56194084}}|given_name={{ill|Naruko|WD=Q110174347}}|resting_place={{ill|Yūten-ji|WD=Q3087698}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8193045]] Wikidata name: Yanagiwara Naruko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Concubine of Emperor Meiji, mother of Emperor Taishō (1859-1943)}} {{family name hatnote|Yanagiwara|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Yanagiwara Naruko | image = Naruko Yanagiwara.png | caption = | title = Imperial Concubine | succession = Imperial Concubine to [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] | reign = 20 February 1873 – 30 July 1912 | birth_date = 26 June 1859 | birth_place = [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1943|10|16|1859|6|26|df=y}} | death_place = | father = Yanagiwara Mitsunaru | mother = | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] | issue = Shigeko, Princess Ume
Yukihito, Prince Take
[[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] | burial_place = [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]], [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], [[:en:Meguro|Meguro]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese]] [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Lady-in-waiting]] of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]]. A [[:en:Concubine|Concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], she was the mother of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] as the second daughter of imperial [[:en:Chamberlain (office)|chamberlain]] [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]''. The [[:en:Yanagiwara family|Yanagiwara family]] were of the [[:en:Reizei family|Reizei family]] line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, [[:en:Count|Count]][https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. [[:en:Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu]] (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō]] and later Governor of [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]]. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the [[:en:Treaty of Shimonoseki|Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty]] after the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of [[:en:Meningitis|Meningitis]] the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. [[:en:Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|Takagi Yuriko]], married her grandson [[:en:Takahito, Prince Mikasa|Prince Mikasa]].{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the [[:en:Hirohito|Showa Emperor]], having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]] (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Sacred Treasure|Order of the Sacred Treasure]] (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Precious Crown|Order of the Precious Crown]] (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}; {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|aristocrat|WD=Q2478141}}; {{ill|courtier|WD=Q1511216}}; {{ill|lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222}}|title={{ill|concubine|WD=Q2719946}}; {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q11573873}}; {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure, 2nd Class|WD=Q56194084}}|given_name={{ill|Naruko|WD=Q110174347}}|resting_place={{ill|Yūten-ji|WD=Q3087698}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8193045]] Wikidata name: Yanagiwara Naruko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Concubine of Emperor Meiji, mother of Emperor Taishō (1859-1943)}} {{family name hatnote|Yanagiwara|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Yanagiwara Naruko | image = Naruko Yanagiwara.png | caption = | title = Imperial Concubine | succession = Imperial Concubine to [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] | reign = 20 February 1873 – 30 July 1912 | birth_date = 26 June 1859 | birth_place = [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1943|10|16|1859|6|26|df=y}} | death_place = | father = Yanagiwara Mitsunaru | mother = | spouse = [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]] | issue = Shigeko, Princess Ume
Yukihito, Prince Take
[[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]] | burial_place = [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]], [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], [[:en:Meguro|Meguro]], [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]] }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a [[:en:Japanese people|Japanese]] [[:en:Lady-in-waiting|Lady-in-waiting]] of the [[:en:Imperial House of Japan|Imperial House of Japan]]. A [[:en:Concubine|Concubine]] of [[:en:Emperor Meiji|Emperor Meiji]], she was the mother of [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] as the second daughter of imperial [[:en:Chamberlain (office)|chamberlain]] [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''[[:en:Chūnagon|Chūnagon]]'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''[[:en:Dainagon|Dainagon]]''. The [[:en:Yanagiwara family|Yanagiwara family]] were of the [[:en:Reizei family|Reizei family]] line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, [[:en:Count|Count]][https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. [[:en:Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu]] (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]] on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the [[:en:Tōkaidō (region)|Tōkaidō]] and later Governor of [[:en:Yamanashi Prefecture|Yamanashi Prefecture]]. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the [[:en:Treaty of Shimonoseki|Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty]] after the [[:en:First Sino-Japanese War|First Sino-Japanese War]], was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of [[:en:Meningitis|Meningitis]] the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future [[:en:Emperor Taishō|Emperor Taishō]]. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. [[:en:Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|Takagi Yuriko]], married her grandson [[:en:Takahito, Prince Mikasa|Prince Mikasa]].{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the [[:en:Hirohito|Showa Emperor]], having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] [[:en:Yūtenji|Yūtenji]] (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Sacred Treasure|Order of the Sacred Treasure]] (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the [[:en:Order of the Precious Crown|Order of the Precious Crown]] (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}; {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|aristocrat|WD=Q2478141}}; {{ill|courtier|WD=Q1511216}}; {{ill|lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222}}|title={{ill|concubine|WD=Q2719946}}; {{ill|lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222}}|children={{ill|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052}}; {{ill|Shigeko, Princess Ume|WD=Q105711297}}; {{ill|Yukihito, Prince Take|WD=Q11065315}}|father={{ill|Yanagiwara Mitsunaru|WD=Q96200289}}|relatives={{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883}}|family={{ill|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139}}|awards={{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q64735577}}; {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q11573873}}; {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure, 2nd Class|WD=Q56194084}}|given_name={{ill|Naruko|WD=Q110174347}}|resting_place={{ill|Yūten-ji|WD=Q3087698}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8193045]] Wikidata name: Yanagiwara Naruko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Concubine of Emperor Meiji, mother of Emperor Taishō (1859-1943)}} {{family name hatnote|Yanagiwara|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Yanagiwara Naruko | image = Naruko Yanagiwara.png | caption = | title = Imperial Concubine | succession = Imperial Concubine to {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} | reign = 20 February 1873 – 30 July 1912 | birth_date = 26 June 1859 | birth_place = {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1943|10|16|1859|6|26|df=y}} | death_place = | father = Yanagiwara Mitsunaru | mother = | spouse = {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} | issue = Shigeko, Princess Ume
Yukihito, Prince Take
{{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}} | burial_place = {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}}, [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], {{ill|Meguro|en|Meguro|WD=Q233903|lt=Meguro}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|WD=Q161652|lt=Japanese}} {{ill|Lady-in-waiting|en|Lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222|lt=Lady-in-waiting}} of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}}. A {{ill|Concubine|en|Concubine|WD=Q2719946|lt=Concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}}, she was the mother of {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} as the second daughter of imperial {{ill|Chamberlain (office)|en|Chamberlain (office)|WD=Q264323|lt=chamberlain}} [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}''. The {{ill|Yanagiwara family|en|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139|lt=Yanagiwara family}} were of the {{ill|Reizei family|en|Reizei family|WD=Q3065463|lt=Reizei family}} line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, {{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}[https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. {{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|en|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883|lt=Yanagiwara Sakimitsu}} (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} and later Governor of {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|WD=Q269655|lt=Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty}} after the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}}, was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of {{ill|Meningitis|en|Meningitis|WD=Q48143|lt=Meningitis}} the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. {{ill|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|en|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|WD=Q1189486|lt=Takagi Yuriko}}, married her grandson {{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|WD=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa}}.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Showa Emperor}}, having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}} (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure|en|Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q1066052|lt=Order of the Sacred Treasure}} (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Precious Crown|en|Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q1065739|lt=Order of the Precious Crown}} (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. {{ill|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|en|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|WD=Q1189486|lt=Takagi Yuriko}}, married her grandson {{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|WD=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa}}.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Showa Emperor}}, having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}} (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure|en|Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q1066052|lt=Order of the Sacred Treasure}} (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Precious Crown|en|Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q1065739|lt=Order of the Precious Crown}} (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

{{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}; {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|WD=Q205662}}|occupation={{ill|aristocrat|WD=Q2478141}}; {{ill|courtier|WD=Q1511216}}; {{ill|lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222}}|title={{ill|concubine|WD=Q2719946}}; {{ill|lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222}}|children={{ill|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052}}; {{ill|Shigeko, Princess Ume|WD=Q105711297}}; {{ill|Yukihito, Prince Take|WD=Q11065315}}|father={{ill|Yanagiwara Mitsunaru|WD=Q96200289}}|relatives={{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883}}|family={{ill|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139}}|awards={{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q64735577}}; {{ill|Grand Cordon of the Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q11573873}}; {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure, 2nd Class|WD=Q56194084}}|given_name={{ill|Naruko|WD=Q110174347}}|resting_place={{ill|Yūten-ji|WD=Q3087698}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q8193045]] Wikidata name: Yanagiwara Naruko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:27, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Concubine of Emperor Meiji, mother of Emperor Taishō (1859-1943)}} {{family name hatnote|Yanagiwara|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox royalty | consort = yes | name = Yanagiwara Naruko | image = Naruko Yanagiwara.png | caption = | title = Imperial Concubine | succession = Imperial Concubine to {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} | reign = 20 February 1873 – 30 July 1912 | birth_date = 26 June 1859 | birth_place = {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, Japan | death_date = {{death date and age|1943|10|16|1859|6|26|df=y}} | death_place = | father = Yanagiwara Mitsunaru | mother = | spouse = {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}} | issue = Shigeko, Princess Ume
Yukihito, Prince Take
{{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}} | burial_place = {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}}, [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], {{ill|Meguro|en|Meguro|WD=Q233903|lt=Meguro}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|WD=Q161652|lt=Japanese}} {{ill|Lady-in-waiting|en|Lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222|lt=Lady-in-waiting}} of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}}. A {{ill|Concubine|en|Concubine|WD=Q2719946|lt=Concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}}, she was the mother of {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} as the second daughter of imperial {{ill|Chamberlain (office)|en|Chamberlain (office)|WD=Q264323|lt=chamberlain}} [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}''. The {{ill|Yanagiwara family|en|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139|lt=Yanagiwara family}} were of the {{ill|Reizei family|en|Reizei family|WD=Q3065463|lt=Reizei family}} line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, {{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}[https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. {{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|en|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883|lt=Yanagiwara Sakimitsu}} (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} and later Governor of {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|WD=Q269655|lt=Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty}} after the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}}, was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of {{ill|Meningitis|en|Meningitis|WD=Q48143|lt=Meningitis}} the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. {{ill|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|en|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|WD=Q1189486|lt=Takagi Yuriko}}, married her grandson {{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|WD=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa}}.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Showa Emperor}}, having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}} (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure|en|Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q1066052|lt=Order of the Sacred Treasure}} (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Precious Crown|en|Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q1065739|lt=Order of the Precious Crown}} (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

A {{ill|Concubine|en|Concubine|WD=Q2719946|lt=Concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}}, she was the mother of {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} as the second daughter of imperial {{ill|Chamberlain (office)|en|Chamberlain (office)|WD=Q264323|lt=chamberlain}} [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}''. The {{ill|Yanagiwara family|en|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139|lt=Yanagiwara family}} were of the {{ill|Reizei family|en|Reizei family|WD=Q3065463|lt=Reizei family}} line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, {{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}[https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. {{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|en|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883|lt=Yanagiwara Sakimitsu}} (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} and later Governor of {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|WD=Q269655|lt=Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty}} after the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}}, was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of {{ill|Meningitis|en|Meningitis|WD=Q48143|lt=Meningitis}} the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. {{ill|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|en|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|WD=Q1189486|lt=Takagi Yuriko}}, married her grandson {{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|WD=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa}}.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Showa Emperor}}, having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}} (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure|en|Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q1066052|lt=Order of the Sacred Treasure}} (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Precious Crown|en|Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q1065739|lt=Order of the Precious Crown}} (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1. '''Lady Yanagiwara Naruko''' |2= 2. Yanagiwara Mitsunaru (1818-1885) |3= 3. Hasegawa Utano (1832-1891) |4= 4. Yanagiwara Takamitsu (1793-1851) |5= 5. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Noriko (1797-1859) |6= 6. Hasegawa Yukiaki |7= |8= 8. Yanagiwara Naomitsu (1772-1812) |9= 9. Lady Ōgimachisanjō Yasuko (1774-1867) |10= 10. [[:ja:正親町三条公則|Ōgimachisanjō Kinnori]] (1774-1800) |11= 11. Kanshūji Makoto |12= |13= |14= |15= }} ==References== {{reflist}} *{{citation|title= Emperor of Japan: Meiji and His World, 1852–1912|last= Keene| first=Donald| author-link =Donald Keene|year= 2002|publisher=Columbia University Press}} ‎]] [[en:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[ca:Yanagihara Naruko]] {{Commons category|Naruko Yanagiwara}} [[es:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[fa:یاناگیوارا ناروکو]] [[fr:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[id:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ja:柳原愛子]] [[jv:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[ko:야나기와라 나루코]] [[sq:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[sv:Yanagiwara Naruko]] [[th:นารูโกะ ยานางิวาระ]] [[vi:Yanagihara Naruko]] [[zh:柳原愛子]] {{wikidata link|Q8193045}} [[Category:1859 births]] [[Category:1943 deaths]] [[Category:19th-century Japanese calligraphers]] [[Category:Emperor Taishō]] [[Category:Fujiwara clan]] [[Category:Grand Cordons of the Order of the Precious Crown]] [[Category:Imperial House of Japan]] [[Category:Japanese concubines]] [[Category:Mothers of Japanese emperors]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Recipients of the Order of the Sacred Treasure]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 22 May 2025]] [[Category:Special:LonelyPages 26 May 2025]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Yukihito, Prince Take
{{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}} | burial_place = {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}}, [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]], {{ill|Meguro|en|Meguro|WD=Q233903|lt=Meguro}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}} }} '''Yanagiwara Naruko''' (Japanese: 柳原愛子), also known as '''Sawarabi no Tsubone''' (26 June 1859 – 16 October 1943), was a {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|WD=Q161652|lt=Japanese}} {{ill|Lady-in-waiting|en|Lady-in-waiting|WD=Q715222|lt=Lady-in-waiting}} of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|WD=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan}}. A {{ill|Concubine|en|Concubine|WD=Q2719946|lt=Concubine}} of {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|WD=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji}}, she was the mother of {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=320.}} and the last concubine to have given birth to a reigning Japanese emperor. ==Life== Yanagiwara Naruko was born in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} as the second daughter of imperial {{ill|Chamberlain (office)|en|Chamberlain (office)|WD=Q264323|lt=chamberlain}} [[Yanagiwara Mitsunaru]] (1818–1885), who held the Rank of ''{{ill|Chūnagon|en|Chūnagon|WD=Q1185382|lt=Chūnagon}}'' in the imperial household and was subsequently appointed ''{{ill|Dainagon|en|Dainagon|WD=Q1354706|lt=Dainagon}}''. The {{ill|Yanagiwara family|en|Yanagiwara family|WD=Q11534139|lt=Yanagiwara family}} were of the {{ill|Reizei family|en|Reizei family|WD=Q3065463|lt=Reizei family}} line of the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Her elder brother, {{ill|Count|en|Count|WD=Q3519259|lt=Count}}[https://books.google.com/books?id=zYpEAQAAMAAJ&dq=Viscount+Yanagihara&pg=PA522 ''The Directory & Chronicle for China, Japan, Corea, Indo-China, Straits Settlements, Malay States, Sian, Netherlands India, Borneo, the Philippines.''], Hongkong Daily Press Office, 1888, p. 322. {{ill|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|en|Yanagiwara Sakimitsu|WD=Q7398883|lt=Yanagiwara Sakimitsu}} (4 May 1850 – 2 September 1894), fought in the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}} on the imperial side, subsequently becoming Lieutenant Governor of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} and later Governor of {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|WD=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. Entering the diplomatic service after the Restoration, he signed the {{ill|Treaty of Shimonoseki|en|Treaty of Shimonoseki|WD=Q269655|lt=Sino-Japanese Friendship treaty}} after the {{ill|First Sino-Japanese War|en|First Sino-Japanese War|WD=Q178687|lt=First Sino-Japanese War}}, was ennobled as a count and became a privy councillor, helping to draft the Imperial House Law before his death at the age of 44. During her lifetime, Lady Naruko was described as an intelligent, graceful and gentle lady, admired by all in the harem.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=250.}} She was noted as an excellent poet and calligrapher. She joined the imperial household in 1870 as a lady-in-waiting to the Dowager [[:en:Empress Eishō|Empress Eishō]], and was appointed ''gon no tenji'' (imperial concubine) on 20 February 1873.{{Harvnb|Keene|2002|p=776.}} Lady Naruko and the Meiji Emperor were both descended from the [[:ja:正親町三条家|Ōgimachisanjō clan]]; both shared a great-great-grandfather in [[:ja:正親町三条実同|Ōgimachisanjō Sanetomo]] (1748–1785), a senior palace courtier, making them third cousins.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e6%9f%b3%e5%8e%9f%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%88%e5%90%8d%e5%ae%b6%ef%bc%89#mitutuna760|title=柳原家（名家） (Yanagihara genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=4 September 2017}} {{in lang|ja}} On 21 January 1875, she gave birth to her first child, Shigeko, Princess Ume, at the Aoyama Palace, but the princess died of {{ill|Meningitis|en|Meningitis|WD=Q48143|lt=Meningitis}} the following year, on 8 June 1876. On 23 September 1877, she gave birth to Yukihito, Prince Take, who also died of meningitis before his first birthday, on 26 July 1878. On 31 August 1879, she gave birth to her third child and the only one to survive into adulthood, the future {{ill|Emperor Taishō|en|Emperor Taishō|WD=Q157052|lt=Emperor Taishō}}. The prolonged and extremely difficult birth resulted in Lady Naruko becoming hysterical, crying and screaming through the delivery. On 6 September, the emperor named his son Yoshihito, later giving him the title of Prince Haru. Owing to the difficult delivery, Lady Naruko did not recover her health for some time; as her son had contracted meningitis shortly after his birth, his life was feared for until he finally recovered in late December. As a result of her delicate health following her son's birth, she never again served the emperor as a physical concubine, though she retained the title. In 1902, she was officially appointed as an imperial lady-in-waiting. In her later years, she was conferred several prestigious honours in recognition of having continued the imperial line, despite a tendency to blame her for her son's increasingly poor mental health. However, she was permitted to be present at her son's deathbed in December 1926. In 1941, Lady Naruko's great-grandniece, the Hon. {{ill|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|en|Yuriko, Princess Mikasa|WD=Q1189486|lt=Takagi Yuriko}}, married her grandson {{ill|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|en|Takahito, Prince Mikasa|WD=Q280229|lt=Prince Mikasa}}.{{cite web|url=https://reichsarchiv.jp/%e5%ae%b6%e7%b3%bb%e3%83%aa%e3%82%b9%e3%83%88/%e9%ab%98%e6%9c%a8%e6%b0%8f#masanari948|title=高木氏 (Takagi genealogy)|website=Reichsarchiv|access-date=6 January 2018}} {{in lang|ja}} Lady Naruko died on 16 October 1943, aged 84, in the reign of her grandson the {{ill|Hirohito|en|Hirohito|WD=Q34479|lt=Showa Emperor}}, having outlived her son the previous emperor by nearly two decades. She was buried in [[:en:Nakameguro, Meguro, Tokyo|Nakameguro]] {{ill|Yūtenji|en|Yūtenji|WD=Q3087698|lt=Yūtenji}} (5-chome, Meguro-ku) in Tokyo. ==Honours== *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Sacred Treasure|en|Order of the Sacred Treasure|WD=Q1066052|lt=Order of the Sacred Treasure}} (10 May 1925; Second Class: 1 December 1915) *Grand Cordon of the {{ill|Order of the Precious Crown|en|Order of the Precious Crown|WD=Q1065739|lt=Order of the Precious Crown}} (11 February 1940) ===Order of precedence=== *Fourth Rank (29 November 1892) *Senior third Rank (July 1913) *Second Rank (1 December 1915) *Senior second Rank (8 May 1919) *First Rank (16 October 1943; posthumous) ==Ancestry== {{ahnentafel |collapsed=yes |align=center |boxstyle_1=background-color: #fcc; |ref= |boxstyle_2=background-color: #fb9; |boxstyle_3=background-color: #ffc; |boxstyle_4=background-color: #bfc; |1= 1.
